speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four. The survivor is Susan Brunn. How long did you work for Peter Pan Brassieres?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I worked there until 1949-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '47, '48, '49, yes. I had a summer job. I worked for them. I took off for two months-- July and August. My mother had a very good friend from Berlin yet, who had a hotel here in the Catskill Mountains, and she offered me a job during July and August-- like these summer jobs in the mountains. And I could take my daughter along, out of the city. I couldn't afford a camp. And so I took that job for the summer, and then I went back to Peter Pan. But they did that only for   three years, and then they said, oh, no. And I didn't want to give up the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the hotel in the Catskills?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hotel Hunter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your daughter at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was-- January of '44-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three years, a little over three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took care of her when you were working at Peter Pan?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had her in a-- in a-- a daycare.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else happened to you in your working career in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to me? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I like my job. I did very well. I made-- at that time, for a woman, just arrived here-- quite a good money since I worked piecework. And matter of fact, I have been told that a couple of times to work a little slower, in order to keep the wages the way they are. But I was new, and I wanted to make as much money as I could. We needed everything. And we even paid back to the HIAS the money for the trips from Germany to here, for the boat. We paid that all back within maybe six, eight months. Until we found out we shouldn't have. It was donation money, and they were not supposed to ask back for  it. But we had paid it back already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of working experiences did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not work for anybody else. I always wanted to be on my own. And my mother had gotten remarried at that point. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we borrowed some money or something also, and we bought a little luncheonette-- a little less than a luncheonette, in Washington Heights. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where we worked, my husband and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the luncheonette?

speaker: interviewee
text: Garden Luncheonette. It was very nice-- except it was a former White Tower. It was that white building, with quite a big piece of property. What I didn't know was that the building belonged to this man we boarded from, but not the ground-- not the-- And one of these days, we had to get out. They said, you can take your building, but we need the ground. So I couldn't take the building on my head and go. So that's how we lost it. But it was very nice, and we made a living-- not a very big living, but a living. I had a very nice little garden in the back, which I fixed up the typical European style-- tables, little lanterns over it. And a lot of young kids came, and they were the best customers. They drank one evening-- like each of them two, three malteds, or ice cream sodas. And they played the jukebox, and they danced. I even have pictures of them yet. We were very good friends. And it was good, until we lost the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did I do then? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I still had my job in the country, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but at a different place-- not at that place anymore. In another hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took my daughter along there, for as long as I could. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Only at the time when we had the luncheonette, and I didn't go to the summer job anymore-- I put her into a camp, a very nice camp, which was, at that point, inexpensive and very nice. And then I didn't work anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get divorced?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1900-- we separated in 1960, and my divorce became final in 1962 in June. And I got remarried in September of the same year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- how did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was very funny. He was with his first wife, a customer of mine, in the hotel where I worked, but we had no connection. And we were invited-- I was invited somewhere, and he was there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we talked about that he just had learned in a school to go and play bridge. And I told him also-- it was a party with many people-- that I just learned to play bridge. So he said, how about playing bridge together once? So I said, fine, come on over one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had another couple here, and we played bridge together. And we were all beginners. And that's how it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married to Kurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: 34 years and three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do with Kurt as a joint business?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the home catering.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of catering did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything, from bat mitzvahs, bar mitzvahs, weddings, birthday parties, but only private-- in homes. Except I had one temple where I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how many years did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We retired in 1990-- I think, 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years were you in business?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 28 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your experiences in any way affected the way you practice Judaism today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. I'm more aware [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] of being Jewish, of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] keeping holidays, and being active in B'nai B'riths, or-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Maybe it has to do with it, I don't know. Maybe it has to do with that I'm married to a man who is more involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your experiences affect in any way the way you raised your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think so, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your mother today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is living with us since 19-- I mean, she lived with us when we got here, when we got to America. And in 1949, I think, she remarried, and her husband unfortunately passed away in 1956, I think. And from then on, she lived with us again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is going to be 99 in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to tell us your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I feel if nobody would do it, then nobody would know about it in later years to come. And this is something which should never be forgotten-- especially since there are people around who say that there was never a Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is an absolute lie. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have lost 80% of my relatives in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add to what we've already said today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't know what. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm just happy that it's over, that we made it, that we are alive, and that we're in this country. Nothing is 100% perfect-- nowhere. But I still think it's the best country to be in. When we go on vacation on a trip, we're always happy to come back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does your daughter live today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter lives in Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Combined with my husband, we have four. But on my own, my blood relations, are two.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julie is 29, and Scott is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 26.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing this day with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. I did it with pleasure. This is my grandmother and grandfather, the parents of my mother. And their name is Israel and Berta Froehlich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture must have been taken around the 1900s, 1895, 1900, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, in Ratibor, yeah. This is my mother and my father. Their name is Helen and Ernest Mendel. And the picture must have been taken somewhere around 1922 in Berlin. And my father was a very nice-looking man, and he was an actor. This is my father, Ernest Mendel, while he was an officer in the German army in the war of 1914 to 1918. This picture must have been taken somewhere before 1918, when he became a prisoner of the British and he was in a prison camp on French soil. This is a picture of myself, which was taken at about age four or five. I don't know exactly how old I was there. And I was living at that time in Ratibor, Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living with my aunt and uncle. This is a picture of myself, at the age of maybe 16. And that was, of course, during the Hitler time in Berlin. This is a picture of an uncle of my husband, and myself, right after we got officially married in 1945, and who presented us at this time with a beautiful, big, fresh fruit basket-- which was very hard to get by, but he somehow got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was taken in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only remember his first name, Uncle Emil. This is my husband, Klaus Wertheim, and our little girl and myself in December of 1946, in Berlin, on the main street, Kurfurstendamm-- which was taken by a street photographer. This is a picture of our daughter, Sylvia, who was at that time two years old. And that picture was taken in the house where we lived at that time in Glienicke. And the carriage I got from a friend of my mother, and I bought it for 10 pounds of potatoes. OK. This is my grandson Scott Zimmerman, who is at this moment a captain in the American army. And he is stationed in Bosnia in Tuzla, and I hope he's going to come home soon. And this picture was taken in Europe, in 1996. OK. This is a family picture of the five generations of our family. From right to left is my husband Kurt, then it's me, then comes my daughter Sylvia, then comes my granddaughter Julie holding her newborn baby. Then is my mother, and at the end is Julie's husband Scott. And it was taken on the occasion of the first visit of the baby to our house, when the baby was one week old. And it was taken in the first week of December 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this new baby is your great-grand--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? OK. This is my husband, Kurt. We are married 34 years and three months. And it has been a very nice, good time together-- and I wouldn't change it a bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Kurt Brunn. And I really appreciate to have such a good wife, after I went through so much in my first marriage. And what she went through in life, I just-- it's impossible to describe. But it's-- I'm amazed that she's still-- that she's still fine there like that. I don't know what to say more. And we love our children, and she loves my children. And we hope we can continue many more years. And one thing for sure is that we both have compassion, and feel for grandma and want to go through life many, many more years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing this with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't prepared really.
